title: Boardwalk Pins
desc: A swamp boardwalk is only a boardwalk for as long as someone keeps insisting on it.

steps.text:
The boardwalk into Gloomuck Swamp begins as a practical statement and ends as an argument with mud. Planks laid over crossbeams, pins driven through the supports, rope handlines fixed where the footing turns treacherous. The swamp answers by softening posts, shifting muck under the braces, and trying to digest the whole arrangement one damp inch at a time.

After last night's rain, three sections have gone loose. A path worker is already there with a mallet, a crate of replacement pins, and a look that says this is not her first swamp and will not be her last. "We fix it now," she says, bouncing once on a suspect plank, "or the next messenger learns to swim in mud."

The repair splits cleanly. The support pins need driving back to depth, or the side handline needs new knots where the wet rope has stretched and sagged.

options.label: Drive the loose support pins back in
Retie the handline along the unstable span

steps.text:
Driving swamp pins is wet, stubborn work. You brace one boot on the plank, line the ironwood peg over its hole, and hammer until the whole walkway shudders under the blow. Mud belches around the support post, dark and foul and vaguely offended to be interrupted.

One by one the loose sections settle. The worker tests each repaired span with careful weight, listening for the right deep answer from the beams underneath. Nearby water insects scatter at every strike, then drift back in glittering little swarms as soon as the ringing stops.

When the last pin seats flush, the boardwalk still looks temporary and probably always will. But it no longer feels negotiable underfoot. The worker gives the repaired stretch a final approving stomp. "Good. Now it can go back to pretending it was always this solid."

options.label: Wipe the mud from the mallet handle

steps.text:
The handline matters most on the sections where the boardwalk narrows and the mud below loses any sense of bottom. You unthread the worst of the swollen rope, pull new line through the side posts, and knot each span tight enough to catch weight without pulling the stanchions inward.

The work runs beside black water topped with green skin. Every so often a bubble rises and breaks with a smell you can taste through your teeth. Beyond the rail, reeds knock quietly against one another while the bird tree stands far off like a witness too dignified to comment.

Once the new line is set, the whole walk changes from invitation to usable path. The worker wraps the old rope into a foul damp coil and drops it in her cart. "Better," she says. "A swamp will never promise you much. A handline should."

options.label: Test the knots and move to firmer ground
